docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
This user guide will demonstrate the basics of working with GitHub using Git. In this documentation we will go over creating a new repository in GitHub, pushing an existing project into it, and working with an existing repository (someone else's code) in GitHub.

Note

GitHub is a website that lets you host git repositories. It is used for collaboration with other developers, storing and reviewing code.

Git is a Version Control System used to track files and changes in your code over time.

docs.title: Intended Users && Prerequisite Knowledge
docs.text: This documentation is intended for individuals who have a basic knowledge of GitHub such as git clone, git status, git add, git commit, git push etc or Version Control Systems such as Git.

docs.title: Software Requirements
docs.text:
The following software(s) must be installed and accounts created:

  • Visual Studio Code
  • Git Bash
  • GitHub account

docs.title: Procedures Overview
docs.text:
This document will cover the following tasks:

  • Connecting Gitbash to Visual Studio Code terminal
  • Creating a new repository in GitHub
  • Pushing an existing project to GitHub
  • Working with existing code on GitHub
  • Cloning
  • Zip download

docs.title: Typographical Conventions
docs.text:
1. There will be no code blocks in this documentation. Screenshots will be used to guide users.
  2. Filenames will be formatted in monospace code format and put in quotes.(package.json)
  3. Action verbs and UI elements will be bold. (Click, Highlight, New Repository etc)

docs.title: Notes and Warning Messages
docs.text:
This documentation will use message blocks to inform you of relevant information

Warning

Specifies content that must be read before proceeding.

Info

Indicates additional information or tips.

Success

Indicates what success looks like.

docs.title: Connecting Git Bash to VS Code Terminal
Overview
docs.text:
In this section, you will learn how to configure and use Git Bash as the integrated terminal inside Visual Studio Code.

Using Git Bash in VS Code allows you to:

  • Use Linux-style commands (ls, cd, etc.)
  • Work consistently with Git workflows
  • Follow tutorials that use Bash commands

If you are already comfortable using another terminal (e.g., PowerShell or Command Prompt), you may skip this section.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure:

  • Git is installed on your computer 👉 You can download it from: https://git-scm.com/
  • Visual Studio Code is installed

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open Visual Studio Code

  2. Double click on Visual Studio Code to open it.

  3. Open the Menu

  4. Click on the three dots at the top left.

  1. Open Terminal Menu
  2. Click on the Terminal option from the dropdown.

  1. Create New Terminal
  2. Select New Terminal.

  1. Locate Terminal Panel

  2. A terminal window should appear at the bottom of VS Code.

  3. Open Terminal Options

  4. Click on the terminal menu.

  1. Open Dropdown Options
  2. Click the dropdown arrow next to the + icon.

  1. Select Git Bash
  2. Check if Git Bash is listed and select it.

docs.title: If Git Bash is NOT Listed
docs.text: 9️. Open Command Palette - Press Ctrl + Shift + P

docs.title: ⭐ Set Git Bash as Default
docs.text:
1. Select Default Profile
  2. Go to Select Default Profile

  3. Choose Git Bash

  4. Open New Terminal

  5. Click New Terminal (+) again

  6. Done!

  7. Git Bash should now run inside VS Code 🎉

Next thing to do is Create new git

docs.title: Glossary table
docs.text: Term Definition Git A distributed version control system used to track changes in code and collaborate with others. GitHub A cloud-based platform that hosts Git repositories and enables collaboration, version control, and project management. Repository (Repo) A storage location for a project’s files and version history, managed using Git. Clone The process of creating a local copy of a remote GitHub repository on your machine. Commit A snapshot of changes made to files in a repository, saved with a message describing the changes. Branch A separate line of development that allows you to work on features or fixes without affecting the main codebase. Main Branch The primary branch of a repository where stable code is typically stored (often named main or master). Remote A version of your repository hosted on a platform like GitHub. Origin The default name given to the remote repository when linking a local repo to GitHub. Push The process of uploading local commits to a remote repository on GitHub. Pull The process of fetching and merging changes from a remote repository into your local repository. Stage (Staging Area) The step where changes are prepared before being committed using git add. Initialize (git init) The process of creating a new Git repository in a local project folder. Terminal A command-line interface used to interact with your system and run Git commands. Git Bash A terminal application for Windows that provides Git command-line features. README A file that describes a project, typically including its purpose, setup instructions, and usage. .gitignore A file that specifies which files or folders Git should ignore and not track. URL (Repository URL) The web address used to access or connect to a GitHub repository. ZIP Download A method of downloading a repository as a compressed file without version control features. Version Control A system that records changes to files over time, allowing you to track history and collaborate efficiently.

docs.title: Creating a New Repository in GitHub
Overview
docs.text:
This guide walks you through the process of creating a new GitHub repository, which is used to store, manage, and track your project files using Git version control.

A repository allows you to:

  • Save your code online
  • Track changes over time
  • Collaborate with others
  • Share your projects publicly or privately

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • A GitHub account → https://github.com/
  • Logged into your account

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Navigate to GitHub
  2. Go to GitHub and sign up or log in.
  1. Click the "+" Icon
  2. Click on the "+" icon in the top-right corner of the navigation bar.
  1. Select "New repository"
  2. Choose New repository from the dropdown.
  1. Choose Repository Owner
  2. Click the dropdown arrow to select a Repository Owner.
  1. Enter Repository Name
  2. Type a name for your repository.
  1. Add Description (Optional)
  2. Provide a short summary explaining the purpose of the repository.

docs.title: Set Repository Visibility
docs.text:
1. Select Visibility
  2. Click on Public.
  1. Choose Visibility Option
  2. Select one:
  3. Public (visible to everyone)
  4. Private (only accessible to you or collaborators)

docs.title: Initialize the Repository (Recommended)
docs.text:
1. Add README
  2. Check the option to add a README file.
  1. Add .gitignore (Optional)
  2. Choose a .gitignore file if needed.

docs.title: Choose a License & Create Repository
docs.text:
1. Select License (Optional)
  2. Choose an open-source license if you want others to use or contribute.
  1. Create Repository
  2. Click the Create repository button.
  1. Done! Your new GitHub repository is ready to use

Next thing to do is Push work to git

docs.title: Pushing an Existing Project to GitHub
Overview
docs.text:
This guide walks you through how to take an existing project on your computer and upload (push) it to a GitHub repository.

By the end of this guide, you will:

  • Initialize Git in your project
  • Track and commit your files
  • Connect your project to GitHub
  • Push your code online for backup and collaboration.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, make sure you have:

  • Git installed → https://git-scm.com/
  • A GitHub account → https://github.com/
  • Your project already created on your computer

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Open Your Project Folder

  2. Navigate to your existing project directory on your computer.

  3. Open a Terminal Choose one of the following:

  4. Right-click inside the folder → Git Bash

  5. Open VS Code → Press `Ctrl + `` to open the terminal

  6. Initialize Git Start tracking your project:

git init
  1. Check Status (Optional) See which files are untracked:
git status
  1. Stage Files Add all files to staging:
git add .
  1. Commit Changes Save your files with a message:
git commit -m "Initial commit"
  1. Create a GitHub Repository
  2. Go to GitHub
  3. Click New Repository
  4. ⚠️ Do NOT initialize with a README if your project already has files

Warning

Do not initialize the repository with a README, .gitignore, or license if you already have a local project.

  1. Copy Repository URL Example:
https://github.com/username/repo.git
  1. Add Remote Origin Link your local project to GitHub:
git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo.git
  1. Verify Remote (Optional) 
git remote -v
  1. Push to GitHub 
git push -u origin main

💡 Use master instead of main if your branch uses that name.

Tip

You can check your branch name using: 

git branch

  1. Confirm Upload
  2. Refresh your GitHub repository page

  3. Ensure all files have been uploaded successfully

  4. Done! Your project is now successfully pushed to GitHub and ready for collaboration 🚀

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Symptom Probable Cause Action Git Bash does not appear in the VS Code terminal profile list Git for Windows is not installed, or VS Code did not detect Git Bash automatically Install Git for Windows, then restart VS Code. If it still does not appear, manually add the Git Bash profile in settings.json. Git Bash opens in VS Code but does not work correctly The Git Bash executable path is incorrect Check that the path points to the correct file, such as C:\Program Files\Git\bin\bash.exe. The terminal opens PowerShell instead of Git Bash Git Bash was not selected as the default terminal profile Open the terminal profile menu in VS Code and set Git Bash as the default profile. git is not recognized as a command Git is not installed or was not added to the system PATH Reinstall Git for Windows and ensure Git is added to PATH during installation. Restart the terminal afterward. Unable to create a new repository on GitHub You are not signed in, or the repository name is already in use Sign in to GitHub and choose a unique repository name. The New repository option is missing You may not be signed in correctly, or you do not have permission in the selected organization Confirm you are signed in to the correct GitHub account and check organization permissions if applicable. git init does not work The terminal is not opened in the correct project folder Navigate to the project directory first, then run git init again. git add . does not stage files You may be in the wrong folder, or there are no files in the directory Run git status to confirm your current folder and check that your project files are present. git commit -m "Initial commit" fails Git username and email are not configured Configure Git with git config --global user.name "Your Name" and git config --global user.email "your@email.com", then try again. remote origin already exists appears A remote repository has already been linked to the local project Check existing remotes with git remote -v. Update it with git remote set-url origin <repository-url> if needed. git push -u origin main fails because the branch does not exist Your local branch may still be named master instead of main Run git branch -M main and then push again, or use the correct existing branch name. Authentication failed when pushing to GitHub Your login credentials are incorrect, expired, or GitHub requires a personal access token Sign in again and use a personal access token if prompted instead of your GitHub password. The repository on GitHub is empty after pushing The push did not complete successfully, or files were never committed Run git status and git log to confirm your files were committed, then push again. git clone fails The repository URL is incorrect, or you do not have permission to access the repository Copy the repository URL again from GitHub and verify that the repository exists and is accessible to your account. The cloned folder does not appear where expected The terminal was opened in a different directory before cloning Run pwd to confirm your location, then clone the repository into the correct folder. Downloaded ZIP file does not include version control features ZIP downloads do not preserve Git history or repository connection Use git clone instead if you need full version control and the ability to pull or push changes. Changes made after downloading a ZIP file cannot be pushed to GitHub The ZIP method does not connect the project to GitHub automatically Initialize Git with git init, add a remote repository, commit your files, and then push them manually.

docs.title: Working with Existing Code on Github
Overview
docs.text:
This section demonstrates how to work with existing code from a GitHub repository. There are two common methods:

  1. Cloning – the easiest and most efficient method
  2. Downloading a ZIP file – a simpler method without version control

docs.title: Steps
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the desired repository on GitHub using your web browser.

  2. Copy the Repository URL

    • Click the green “Code” button
    • Copy the HTTPS URL

  3. Open a Terminal

    • Open Git Bash, or
    • Open Visual Studio Code and launch the terminal

  4. Choose a Local Directory

    • Navigate to the folder where you want to store the project:

    cd path/to/your/folder

  5. Clone the Repository

    • Run the following command:

    git clone https://github.com/username/repo.git

  6. Navigate into the Project Folder

    cd repo-name

  7. Verify Files Were Downloaded

    • Check that all project files are present in the directory.

  8. Check Repository Status (Optional)

    git status

  9. Open the Project in an Editor

    • Open the folder in Visual Studio Code or your preferred IDE.

  10. Start Working on the Code

    • Make changes, add features, or fix bugs as needed.

  11. Pull Latest Changes (Recommended)

    • Before starting work, ensure your code is up to date:

    git pull

docs.title: Method 2: Downloading as a ZIP File
Steps
docs.text:
1. Navigate to the Repository

    • Open the repository on GitHub.

  2. Click the “Code” Button

    • This will open a dropdown menu.

  3. Select “Download ZIP”

    • The repository will be downloaded as a compressed file.

  4. Locate the Downloaded File

    • Find the ZIP file in your Downloads folder.

  5. Extract the ZIP File

    • Right-click the file
    • Select “Extract All”

  6. Open the Extracted Folder

    • Navigate into the unzipped project directory.

  7. Open in an Editor

    • Open the folder using Visual Studio Code or another IDE.

  8. Review the Project Files

    • Ensure all files are accessible and intact.

  9. Make Changes Locally

    • Edit the code as needed.

  10. Initialize Git (optional)

    • If you want version control:
